notes.note: Phrenology was a popular nineteenth-century pseudo-science that claimed the bumps and shape of the skull revealed a person's character and intelligence. Ishmael reads Queequeg's head by its rules and pronounces it noble, borrowing the era's fashionable scientific language to dignify the friend others would dismiss as a savage.

notes.anchor: do to my fellow man what I would have my fellow man to do to me
notes.note: This is the Golden Rule of Matthew 7:12, that you should do to others as you would have them do to you. Ishmael reasons from Christ's own command to justify joining Queequeg in his worship, turning a central Christian teaching against Christian exclusiveness.

Tartar, a variant of Tatar, named the nomadic peoples of the Central Asian steppes and carried a sense of wild, bracing fierceness in nineteenth-century English. Ishmael uses it to catch the sharp, exhilarating cold of the salt wind as the little schooner stands out to sea.

The Book of Job ends with God describing Leviathan, a sea-beast of such power that no man can master it, as proof of a majesty past human reach (Job 41). Ishmael claims Job as the earliest author to write of the great whale, giving the rough trade of whaling the authority of one of the oldest books in the Bible.

notes.anchor: Cetus is a constellation in the South
notes.note: Cetus is the sea-monster of Greek myth that Poseidon sends to devour Andromeda before the hero Perseus kills it, a story told in Bulfinch's Age of Fable; the same beast was set among the stars as the constellation Cetus. Ishmael's argument is that even the night sky honors the whale, so whaling shares in that ancient glory.

notes.anchor: head is solemnly oiled at his coronation
notes.note: Anointing a ruler with oil is an old biblical rite: Samuel pours oil on Saul's head to make him king (1 Samuel 10:1), and Solomon is anointed at Gihon (1 Kings 1:39); British coronations kept the custom into modern times. The chapter's joke is that the finest such oil is sperm-whale oil, so whalemen secretly consecrate every king.

Macassar oil was a fashionable Victorian hair-dressing, marketed as coming from Makassar in the East Indies and sold widely in Britain from the early 1800s. Ishmael ranks it among the lesser oils unworthy of a royal head, part of the comic case that only sperm oil is fine enough for a coronation.
